cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A global network of interconnected computers and devices that communicate using agreed-upon protocols

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A system of web pages and documents connected by hyperlinks and accessed via the Internet

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A small unit of data that is transmitted over a network; large messages are broken into many of these

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A unique numerical label assigned to each device on a network to identify and locate it

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: The older IP addressing system using 32-bit addresses, supporting about 4.3 billion unique addresses

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: The newer IP addressing system using 128-bit addresses, supporting a vastly larger number of unique addresses

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A connection-oriented protocol that ensures all packets are delivered reliably and in order

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A connectionless protocol that sends packets quickly without guaranteeing delivery or order — used for streaming and gaming

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A system that translates human-readable domain names (like google.com) into IP addresses

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A protocol for transmitting web pages over the Internet without encryption

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A protocol for transmitting web pages over the Internet with encryption for security

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A networking device that forwards packets between networks by determining the best path for each packet

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: The maximum rate at which data can be transferred over a network connection, measured in bits per second

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: The delay between sending data and receiving it at the destination

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A set of agreed-upon rules that govern how computers communicate over a network

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: The ability of a network to continue functioning even when one or more components fail

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: Having extra components or paths so that a system can keep working if one part fails

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A computing model where multiple processors work on different parts of a task simultaneously

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A computing model where tasks are completed one step at a time in order

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A computing model that uses multiple networked computers to solve a large problem by dividing the work

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: The ability of a system to handle increasing amounts of work by adding resources

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A networking method where data is broken into packets that each independently find their way to the destination, then are reassembled

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: The use of publicly available specifications and standards so anyone can build compatible systems

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: TCP vs. UDP
cards.answer: TCP guarantees reliable, ordered delivery of data (used for email, web); UDP is faster but unreliable (used for video streaming, gaming)

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: Computing device
cards.answer: A physical artifact that can run programs, such as a computer, smartphone, tablet, or smart sensor

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: Computer network
cards.answer: A group of interconnected computing devices that can communicate and share data with each other

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: Which protocol would be better for a video call application where some lost frames are acceptable but low delay is critical?

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: Which protocol would be better for downloading a file where every byte must be received correctly?

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A task takes 60 seconds sequentially. If 3 processors each handle one third of the work in parallel, what is the minimum possible time?

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: The layers of protocols and abstractions that allow different hardware and software systems to communicate on the Internet

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: A network with no central authority — no single point of control or failure

cards.subject: ap-csp
cards.prompt: Why is the Internet considered fault tolerant?
cards.answer: Because packets can take multiple paths to reach their destination, so the network continues to function even if some nodes fail
alternate_answers: multiple paths exist for data routing
redundant paths allow routing around failures
